author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Holosim is very impressive, no question asked. But in the meantime I have some concerns: In Holosim you are progressing very fast and you don't need to buy any assets. Also Holosim itself is very responsive and fast, because it runs on a dedicated Solana fork. Furthermore ships are moving 5x faster, mining rate is up to 10x higher. No fiddling around with a wallet software, it works out-of-the box. You can use all features of the "real" Sage and you have fun playing the game. If you want to switch to the "real" Sage, you would lose all your progress. So my question is: Why would anyone in their right mind switch from Holosim to Sage? The only thing you cannot do on Holosim: Export assets to your wallet and trade them outside the game for money. But you can trade anything in the game. And if there's one thing I've learned: Players will always find a way to circumvent restrictions. E.g. OTC deals with a trusted middleman (I give you real money, you give me ingame assets via an instantly accepted fake trade offer). Maybe I missed something? Where is the big incentive to play the real Sage? In case I didn't miss anything, a simple idea: Holosim isn't permanent. The game completely resets after each season (e.g. after 3 months).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I've been coming at it heavy from the game system/design tooling angle and it's been a huge accelerant to the output and manageable scale. Basically, I spend an afternoon laying the groundwork for a specific tool for our needs, structure it ingest some existing data and build upon the capabilities of the editor to structure deeper and deeper layers of data with handcraft tooling to expand the amount of data that ends up in the game. I'll have cursor on one screen and the editor on another and as I'm inputting data manually and any time I do something repetitive I'll hop back to cursor to ask it to build a tool to handle that repetition. This is the acceleration that is kind of head spinning. By the time I'm done with features I can utilize I'm about done with the output files and have shaved weeks and months off refining the data. And also have a nifty tool to put in our suite should I ever need to hand it off to econ or another designer to make edits or receive updates from. I've spent probably a collective week of time solo developing the tools I've shown on the TH and also others that have sped up random things along the way. It also helps for prototyping a game design to validate assumptions and allow for easy tweaks.   When I was first using it I was backing up code almost after every prompt but these models have become so good now that I can just restore checkpoint if need be but even that has gotten exceedingly rare. I feel like if we don't capitalize on this level of multiplication of solo output now then everyone will soon be on our tail. Building a game at this scale is becoming increasingly more viable for small teams to compete with massive studios who spend years refining these kinds of systems. It's a complete paradigm shift. And speaking from this angle only is a small portion of the larger picture here. Jacob and Chip could go into much greater detail on how their teams have also utilized AI to boost productivity. Anyway, I could go on and on how head spinning this is. For example just today I added about 30 drone types in the loot matrix, then updated the ship configurator to support drone mechanics and introduces a whole new set of editor functions to quickly swap between ships and configs in the config editor screen. greatly speeding up my addition of drones as i inspect all 61 ships for drone ports and adding them to their configs. All these are one shot additions. In less than a day. No need to manually do this and allows for editing at any point in the chain we need to go back and fuss with any of the values.

When I was first using it I was backing up code almost after every prompt but these models have become so good now that I can just restore checkpoint if need be but even that has gotten exceedingly rare. I feel like if we don't capitalize on this level of multiplication of solo output now then everyone will soon be on our tail. Building a game at this scale is becoming increasingly more viable for small teams to compete with massive studios who spend years refining these kinds of systems. It's a complete paradigm shift. And speaking from this angle only is a small portion of the larger picture here. Jacob and Chip could go into much greater detail on how their teams have also utilized AI to boost productivity. Anyway, I could go on and on how head spinning this is. For example just today I added about 30 drone types in the loot matrix, then updated the ship configurator to support drone mechanics and introduces a whole new set of editor functions to quickly swap between ships and configs in the config editor screen. greatly speeding up my addition of drones as i inspect all 61 ships for drone ports and adding them to their configs. All these are one shot additions. In less than a day. No need to manually do this and allows for editing at any point in the chain we need to go back and fuss with any of the values.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: Thanks man. It was an action packed week. And I'm excited to add in-game POLIS utility as we emerge out of C4 development.  I think it's probably necessary to set a baseline for our expectations for conversion to mainnet. Which, to be fair, are low. If we converted even 5% of Holosim users to mainnet, that would be a win. But at a minimum, it is going to serve as a formidable tool in simply teaching the basics of SAGE gameplay with a very low barrier to entry, and zero risk (aside from a little time invested). That alone makes it a valuable tool in our arsenal for user acquisition and conversion. Separately, as low as that conversion target may be, the real goal here is to create a massive top of funnel. Benchmarking against some other "web3" games like Pixels Online and a few other games on Ronin (which I see as a target for Holosim), they're boasting 1M DAU. I'm not sure if those numbers are still accurate, but they've seen traction largely because of the simplicity of their offering. So from that perspective, if we could bring 1M new people into SA via Holosim and convert even 1% of them, we're looking at 10,000 new DAU -- 10x our current numbers. And then similarly, we have a huge opportunity to promote this across multiple platforms (web and mobile) to a non-crypto native. It's then on us to build the bridges and incentives to get into mainnet gameplay with real economic incentives, which I strongly believe can happen.  And finally, ALL of that aside, we're going to be adding a number of web2 monetization hooks into Holosim. Now, if I benchmark Holosim against something like Star Trek Fleet Command, the numbers are promising. They were making around $15M per month in revenue with estimates of (upwards of) 500,000 DAU. If we achieved even a fraction of that success, it creates significant recurring cash flows for ATMTA that helps ensure we achieve our vision of this decentralized universe. So it's as much a revenue diversification play as it is user acquisition.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis GreatMUD
content: Thanks man. It was an action packed week. And I'm excited to add in-game POLIS utility as we emerge out of C4 development.  I think it's probably necessary to set a baseline for our expectations for conversion to mainnet. Which, to be fair, are low. If we converted even 5% of Holosim users to mainnet, that would be a win. But at a minimum, it is going to serve as a formidable tool in simply teaching the basics of SAGE gameplay with a very low barrier to entry, and zero risk (aside from a little time invested). That alone makes it a valuable tool in our arsenal for user acquisition and conversion. Separately, as low as that conversion target may be, the real goal here is to create a massive top of funnel. Benchmarking against some other "web3" games like Pixels Online and a few other games on Ronin (which I see as a target for Holosim), they're boasting 1M DAU. I'm not sure if those numbers are still accurate, but they've seen traction largely because of the simplicity of their offering. So from that perspective, if we could bring 1M new people into SA via Holosim and convert even 1% of them, we're looking at 10,000 new DAU -- 10x our current numbers. And then similarly, we have a huge opportunity to promote this across multiple platforms (web and mobile) to a non-crypto native. It's then on us to build the bridges and incentives to get into mainnet gameplay with real economic incentives, which I strongly believe can happen.  And finally, ALL of that aside, we're going to be adding a number of web2 monetization hooks into Holosim. Now, if I benchmark Holosim against something like Star Trek Fleet Command, the numbers are promising. They were making around $15M per month in revenue with estimates of (upwards of) 500,000 DAU. If we achieved even a fraction of that success, it creates significant recurring cash flows for ATMTA that helps ensure we achieve our vision of this decentralized universe. So it's as much a revenue diversification play as it is user acquisition.

author: Xap✨ [STAR]
content: @SW4GN3R With upcoming C4 CS will be a huge factor in the game. Previously it was expected that the player can upgrade the lower tier to a higher tier CS. That was a very attractive game loop that is not available now. @bunthius shared that that it may come the future but due to current state and needs of the project that is not possible. I understand the point. Therefore may I suggest that the team adds redeeming recipes in the game that allow obtaining the higher tier CS over time? This will essentially remove any notion of P2W from this topic. One can grind Atlas and eventually purchase a high level CS or they can grind and provide the resources to redeem the CS. I understand that USDC based purchases help the team a lot but I am hoping that having these recipes will also the benefit the game in general.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Yes there will be an on-chain key/account for each system,We used to have sub-coordinates within a system for each. We intended to use those on-chain but never ended up doing it. With star-frame now we can easily add data so we aren't including them on-chain anymore. I believe @bunthius is designing them in though, so they will be obtainable from a non-on-chain source.,Currently static, actually worked out a way to have them move back when SAGE was called SCREAM but we haven't used them on-chain yet,Asteroids will not be random nor will deplete. This is planned work for the future. For now they are placed,Border lines are calced off-chain then uploaded on-chain as polylines. Currently we were running a convex hull on the region to find the general shape but borders are going to be pushed out. Running something like this would be cool.,Warp Lanes are defined as connections between system accounts.,

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I know this isn’t exactly a game-related question, but I think it’s still worth asking.

With crypto assets facing increasing regulation worldwide, there are some situations where a player buying an asset in USDC might be considered as taking profit — for example, the EU MiCA regulation clearly treats USDC as financially relevant and equivalent to fiat, while it’s still quite vague when it comes to USDT.
Meanwhile, converting ATLAS to SOL isn’t considered financially relevant.

Of course, for small amounts this isn’t really an issue, but for higher-tier investments, this might actually influence a player’s decision.

So I was wondering:
Is there any precaution we could take — as a crypto project — to give players more flexibility in how they purchase assets?
Something like allowing purchases using any token (SOL / ATLAS / POLIS / USDC), but with the team still receiving USDC on the backend (maybe charging the player for the fees, which is still less heavy than paying the profit over the amount)?

I know this isn’t a critical issue and shouldn’t divert focus from core development, but I figured it was worth bringing up.

Thanks again!

author: Lorddo [ALRT]
content: Well, we do allow for transactions in both USDC and ATLAS right now. Otherwise, it's unlikely we're in any position to tailor the product to the various tax regimes all around the world. Will have to leave that responsibility to the player. In some distant future, it would be nice for us to be able to provide a version of an in-game P&L to the players so they'd at least have a report to work from with their CPAs or accountants. But certainly not going to be a priority for us near term.

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content: One of the purpose of fleet rental is to create more users by planning for users who do not own fleets to rent fleets at an affordable price so that they can play the game they want.  However, only one user (HN8vWVJxw49VRJCg5H17X448V884bE3LsgjGyBsS8LjK) monopolizes all rent scanning ships in the ONI camp.   This behavior blocks potential entry for users who want to play SCAN from the ONI camp. (He has a monopoly on all Scan fleets within break-even point.)  His behavior itself is not illegal, but when someone monopolizes all ships in this way, it turns into a market for only a few users.  We must not forget the basic purpose of fleet rental systems to expand users.  There is also a way to change the way you rent, but personally, I think it would be wiser to limit one user from monopolizing all reasonably priced fleet(no losses)s.  Who would want to rent a ship at the expense of the loss?

author: Narr_nEoNONI
content:
Mature economic markets regulate these monopolies. As SA also aims to build a mature economic loop, I think there should be some kind of penalty for these monopolies. (Limiting rental quantities, increasing fees for exclusive users, etc.)

Until team make a good product, are you going to leave them alone? Existing users are leaving without new users coming in.

I think it can be a problem that can be solved in a short time without fundamental changes in the system.

It's annoying to think about sniping a fleet of people being listed in a second. Should I watch it repeat itself in the future?

We all know the address of exclusive users. It's not difficult to regulate them. We need to consider what's holding back the mature growth of the SA economy.

An economy that neglects monopolies always fails.

author: Matt | IntergalacticHerald
content: Buildings are not tokens.  Let's say we have a solar power plane building defined to have a cost of 100 Silicon and 10s to build, when you go to your claim stake and want to add that building to it you open the designer, and add 10 of the building. You'll see the net cost of adding that building is 1000 Silicon and when you commit that design it will take 1000 Silicon out of the local starbase and the claim stake will start constructing. Once the construction is complete (100s) the claimstake will then begin operating.  When you want to deconstruct that building, either for changing it out for another or getting the resources back you, you open the designer again. You choose to remove 5 solar power plants. The claim stake goes into construction again (50s). Following this you receive the refund amount (let's say 90 silicon for a solar power plant, so 450 silicon) at your local starbase.   If the claim stake is evicted all the building resources are lost and the claim stake token respawns at the local starbase (if owned by your faction) or the CSS (if local starbase is not owned by the local faction).

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
@SW4GN3R @Xcode @⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK 
Alright. Just had an intense moment in Holosim, chasing people, being chased, almost getting stranded deep into enemy territory (went back home with 3% fuel), after a rogue ONI decided it would be fun to hunt peaceful miners deep inside Ustur territory. Capital fleets involved, a LOT of strategic warping/chasing/luring...

Absolutely freaking AMAZING experience. Which brings up the main point: I know Holosim is a very rough test grounds for how combat will be in C4, and if I'm getting such a great experience already in Holosim, I can't imagine how absurdly cool C4 will be.

Hats off to everyone involved. I tagged the 2 guys active in the Holosim chat room, and Michael, but please extend this to all team members.

Anyone not trying out the combat experience in Holosim is missing out, BIG time.
